title: These Baby Birds Look Like Tiny Rock Stars!
summary:
Imagine three tiny birds that look like they're in a punk rock band. That's what the winning photo of the Smithsonian Magazine Photography Contest shows! The baby egrets have spiky feathers that look just like mohawks. The photographer, Jacqueline Burke, is a 64-year-old veterinarian. She took the picture at a rookery (a place where birds breed and raise babies) in New Jersey. "They were squabbling nonstop," she said. The chicks were no bigger than golf balls when curled up!

The contest got over 17,000 photos from 108 countries. Six other pictures also won prizes. One shows a woman in a black veil. Another has a 14-year-old girl on a horse during a Navajo ceremony. There's also a colorful chameleon and a huge building shaped like a ring. A photographer from South Africa took the chameleon picture. She usually takes photos of endangered animals like African wild dogs and rhinos. But this time, she just thought the chameleon was "really, really beautiful." All the winners make you want to look closer and see the cool details.
why_it_matters: This story shows that ordinary moments in nature can be amazing, and taking time to look closely can help us appreciate the world around us.

background_read: Egrets are a type of heron that often have white feathers.
Many people enjoy watching birds and taking pictures of them.
Photography contests like this one help people see amazing moments in nature.
Article_Structure: WHAT: Took a winning photo of three baby egrets that look like punk rock stars
WHERE: At a rookery (bird breeding place) in Ocean City, New Jersey
WHY: The photo won because of the spiky feathers that look like mohawks and the cute squabbling chicks

perspectives.description: Loves seeing cute animals in their natural habitat and thinks this photo is a great way to celebrate wildlife.
Appreciates the skill of capturing a perfect moment and enjoys seeing the winning entries.
Thinks the contest is mostly for fun, but wonders if it really helps protect wildlife as the photographer hopes.
